topic: Data Preprocessing: Handling Outliers, Missing Values, and Skewed Data

questions.scenario: Real-world Industrial Problem Statement: An e-commerce platform's recommendation engine is training a gradient boosting model on transaction history. The target variable and several key features (such as 'total spend' and 'session duration') are highly right-skewed and contain numerous zero values (for users who browsed but didn't buy) as well as negative values (representing promo discounts and refunds). Standard logarithmic transformation cannot handle these negative and zero values directly.
questions.question: Which transformation strategy is mathematically valid and most effective for stabilizing variance and reducing skewness in this dataset without discarding negative/zero values?
options: A) Apply a standard Logarithmic Transformation (log(x)) after adding a tiny epsilon value (e.g., 1e-5) to all features.
B) Use the Box-Cox transformation directly on the raw features to automatically determine the optimal lambda parameter.
C) Apply the Yeo-Johnson transformation directly to the features to accommodate zero and negative values while stabilizing variance.
D) Utilize a RobustScaler to scale features based on the interquartile range (IQR) to completely eliminate skewness.
questions.correct_answer_text: C) Apply the Yeo-Johnson transformation directly to the features to accommodate zero and negative values while stabilizing variance.
questions.explanation: The Yeo-Johnson transformation is a modification of the Box-Cox transformation that allows for zero and negative values. Box-Cox requires strictly positive values (x > 0). While adding an arbitrary constant shift can allow Box-Cox or standard log transforms to run, it distorts the underlying distribution, especially when zero/negative densities are high. RobustScaler only rescales the range using IQR to mitigate outlier influence but does not alter the underlying skewness or shape of the distribution.

questions.scenario: Real-world Industrial Problem Statement: A cybersecurity firm is deploying an inline network Intrusion Detection System (IDS) that processes high-dimensional network packet metadata (120+ continuous and categorical features) in real-time. The processing pipeline must meet an ultra-low latency threshold of less than 5ms per packet. Outliers, which represent potential zero-day exploits, must be detected immediately on streaming data.
questions.question: Which outlier detection algorithm balances high-dimensional complexity and real-time execution constraints best?
options: A) Local Outlier Factor (LOF) using Euclidean distance across all 120 features to locate local density anomalies.
B) Isolation Forest (iForest) built with a limited number of estimators and sub-sampling, applied to the high-dimensional space.
C) Running t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding (t-SNE) to compress features to 2 dimensions, followed by Mahalanobis distance calculation.
D) An RBF-kernel One-Class Support Vector Machine (OC-SVM) fitted dynamically on each incoming mini-batch of packet streams.
questions.correct_answer_text: B) Isolation Forest (iForest) built with a limited number of estimators and sub-sampling, applied to the high-dimensional space.
questions.explanation: Isolation Forest isolating anomalies rather than profiling normal points is highly efficient. It has a training complexity of O(t * psi * log(psi)) and a prediction complexity of O(t * d) where d is the depth, making it incredibly fast for real-time inference on high-dimensional data. LOF is computationally expensive (O(n^2)) due to nearest neighbor searches. t-SNE is non-parametric, extremely slow, and cannot project new out-of-sample points in real-time. Fitting an RBF-kernel OC-SVM dynamically per mini-batch introduces unacceptable computational overhead for a <5ms latency budget.

questions.scenario: Real-world Industrial Problem Statement: A healthcare provider is building a predictive diagnostic model for cardiovascular risk. Clinical records have high rates of missing values: 'Income Status' is Missing Not at Random (MNAR) because low-income patients systematically omit this information, while 'Serum Cholesterol' is Missing at Random (MAR) because a clinician forgot to order the lab test. The downstream classifier is a sensitive, regularized logistic regression model.
questions.question: Which data preprocessing pipeline handles these distinct missingness mechanisms most effectively without introducing systematic bias?
options: A) Apply Listwise Deletion to remove all records with any missing fields to guarantee only pristine clinical records are used for training.
B) Perform Mean Imputation for 'Serum Cholesterol' and Median Imputation for 'Income Status' to preserve sample size and minimize structural variance changes.
C) Use Multiple Imputation by Chained Equations (MICE) for 'Serum Cholesterol', and create a binary missingness indicator column alongside a placeholder imputation for 'Income Status'.
D) Apply K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) imputation using Euclidean distance across all features globally to impute both variables simultaneously.
questions.correct_answer_text: C) Use Multiple Imputation by Chained Equations (MICE) for 'Serum Cholesterol', and create a binary missingness indicator column alongside a placeholder imputation for 'Income Status'.
questions.explanation: MICE is highly effective for MAR ('Serum Cholesterol') as it models each feature with missing values as a function of other features in a semi-parametric way. However, for MNAR ('Income Status'), the missingness itself contains valuable predictive signals (systematic omission). Simply imputing it would destroy this pattern. By introducing a binary missingness indicator variable, the model can capture the unobserved relationship associated with the missing values, preserving both statistical validity and predictive power.
